prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
Think of attention as a “smart find-and-replace” that runs inside the transformer.

1. Every word (or sub-word) is turned into a vector, like a row in a spreadsheet.  
2. For each word, the model builds three helper vectors from that row:  
   • Query – “What am I looking for?”  
   • Key – “What info do I offer?”  
   • Value – “The actual data I carry.”  
3. To update the vector for word A, the model compares A’s Query against every word’s Key, producing a score that says “how relevant is word B to A?”  
4. These scores are turned into percentages (softmax) and used as weights to blend all the Value vectors.  
5. The blended result replaces A’s vector with a context-aware version.

Because this happens for every word in parallel, the transformer can “pay attention” to any part of the sentence in one shot, instead of reading left-to-right like older models. Multi-head attention simply runs several of these lookups at once, each focusing on different kinds of relationships (syntax, semantics, etc.).
